answer:
**The *Vīramitrodaya* (specifically its *Samayaprakāśa* section on time-reckoning for rites and festivals) by Mitra Miśra repeatedly closes subsections of its sole chapter with this colophon.**[^1]

It names Mitra Miśra as the compiler (son of Paraśurāma Miśra and grandson of Haṃsa Paṇḍita), credits King Vīrasiṃhadeva (son of Mahārāja Madhukarasāha and grandson of Pratāparudra) as the patron—with the precise description "the sun causing the lotus of the earth encircled by the four oceans to bloom"—and praises the compiler as "a lion to the great elephant of the world's poverty, the very life of the learned". This appears at least five times (e.g., after discussions of *tithi*-rules, Janmāṣṭamī, festival days, *śrāddha*-timing, and the full *Samayaprakāśa*).[^2]

**One full instance** (ending the Janmāṣṭamī subsection):

> Thus ends the determination of Janmāṣṭamī in the *Samayaprakāśa* of the treatise named *Vīramitrodaya*, composed by the honorable Mitra Miśra—son of the honorable Paraśurāma Miśra, who was the son of the honorable Haṃsa Paṇḍita; who was prompted by the glorious King Vīrasiṃhadeva, who was the sun causing the lotus of the earth encircled by the four oceans to bloom, son of the glorious Mahārāja Madhukarasāha, who was the son of the glorious Mahārājādhirāja Pratāparudra; whose lotus feet are illuminated by the rays from the crest-jewels of the entire circle of vassal kings; who is a lion to the great elephant of the world's poverty, the very life of the learned, and proficient in crossing the ocean of all lores.[^3]

[^1]: *Vīramitrodaya*, ch. 1 (*Samayaprakāśa*), [overview](https://falsafa.philosophytexts.org/works/unknown-viramitrodaya-d4b632/01/translation/).
[^2]: *Vīramitrodaya*, ch. 1, [p-e1f944](https://falsafa.philosophytexts.org/works/unknown-viramitrodaya-d4b632/01/translation/#p-e1f944), [p-e738e7](https://falsafa.philosophytexts.org/works/unknown-viramitrodaya-d4b632/01/translation/#p-e738e7), [p-e620e4](https://falsafa.philosophytexts.org/works/unknown-viramitrodaya-d4b632/01/translation/#p-e620e4), [p-c59e1b](https://falsafa.philosophytexts.org/works/unknown-viramitrodaya-d4b632/01/translation/#p-c59e1b), [p-d61edd](https://falsafa.philosophytexts.org/works/unknown-viramitrodaya-d4b632/01/translation/#p-d61edd).
[^3]: *Vīramitrodaya*, ch. 1, [p-e738e7](https://falsafa.philosophytexts.org/works/unknown-viramitrodaya-d4b632/01/translation/#p-e738e7).
